Aug 25th 2025 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Dear Dr. Michele Hartsough, We are pleased to submit our manuscript, titled “Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian 
cancer
 
uncovers
 
MHC
 
class
 
II
 
as
 
a
 
key
 
predictor
 
of
 
spatial
 
tumor
 
ecosystems
 
and
 
clinical
 
outcomes
,”
 
for
 
consideration
 
in
 
Cancer
 
Discovery
.
 
This
 
work
 
presents
 
a
 
clinically
 
and
 
molecularly
 
resolved
 
single-cell
 
spatial
 
atlas
 
of
 
280
 
metastatic
 
ovarian
 
cancers,
 
integrating
 
spatial
 
proteomics,
 
genomics,
 
bulk,
 
single-cell,
 
and
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
to
 
map
 
the
 
tumor-immune
 
microenvironment
 
(TME)
 
at
 
unprecedented
 
scale
 
and
 
resolution.
 
 
As
 
discussed
 
in
 
the
 
email
 
(25th
 
June
 
2025),
 
we
 
have
 
now
 
included
 
supporting
 
functional
 
data
 
from
 
our
 
patient-derived
 
organoid
 
platform,
 
and
 
we
 
would
 
be
 
grateful
 
for
 
your
 
feedback
 
on
 
the
 
manuscript.
 
 High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC), the most lethal gynecologic malignancy, has shown limited 
responses
 
to
 
immunotherapy.
 
Prior
 
work
 
by
 
our
 
group
 
and
 
others
 
has
 
demonstrated
 
that
 
tumor-immune
 
interactions
 
drive
 
responses
 
to
 
chemotherapy
 
(
Launonen
 
2021
 
Nat
 
Commun
;
 
Launonen
 
2024
 
Cancer
 
Cell
)
 
and
 
immunotherapy
 
combinations
 
(
Färkkilä
 
2019
 
Nat

s
 
drive
 
responses
 
to
 
chemotherapy
 
(
Launonen
 
2021
 
Nat
 
Commun
;
 
Launonen
 
2024
 
Cancer
 
Cell
)
 
and
 
immunotherapy
 
combinations
 
(
Färkkilä
 
2019
 
Nat
 
Commun
).
 
However,
 
improving
 
therapeutic
 
strategies
 
and
 
patient
 
stratification
 
requires
 
a
 
comprehensive
 
understanding
 
of
 
how
 
the
 
tumor
 
ecosystem
 
is
 
orchestrated
 
in
 
HGSC.
 In this study, we present a multi-omics, spatially resolved analysis of the TME in metastatic HGSC with a 
total
 
number
 
of
 
15.1
 
million
 
cells
 
across
 
multiomic
 
datasets.
 
Using
 
4.6
 
million
 
single
 
cells
 
across
 
929
 
high-dimensional
 
maps,
 
we
 
identify
 
distinct
 
spatial
 
domains
 
that
 
correspond
 
to
 
clinicomolecular
 
subtypes
 
and
 
are
 
predictive
 
of
 
clinical
 
outcomes.
 
To
 
uncover
 
key
 
features
 
of
 
these
 
tumor
 
ecosystems,
 
we
 
developed
 
CEFIIRA
 
(Cell
 
Feature
 
Importance
 
Identification
 
by
 
Random
 
Forest)—a
 
versatile,
 
open-source
 
machine-learning
 
pipeline.
 
This
 
tool
 
robustly
 
identifies
 
clinically
 
relevant
 
features,
 
such
 
as
 
tumor-intrinsic
 
MHC-II
 
expression,
 
which
 
we
 
found
 
to
 
be
 
a
 
critical
 
driver
 
of
 
prolonged
 
survival.
 
Tumor-intrinsic
 
MHC-II
 
also
 
mediates
 
immune
 
infiltration,
 
antigen
 
presentation,
 
and
 
responses
 
to
 
immunotherapy
 
in
 
vitro

er
 
of
 
prolonged
 
survival.
 
Tumor-intrinsic
 
MHC-II
 
also
 
mediates
 
immune
 
infiltration,
 
antigen
 
presentation,
 
and
 
responses
 
to
 
immunotherapy
 
in
 
vitro
,
 
providing
 
novel
 
insights
 
into
 
the
 
immune
 
surveillance
 
mechanisms
 
across
 
HGSC
 
subtypes.
 Key Novelties and Impact of Our Work: 
1.
 
Unprecedented
 
Scale
 
and
 
Resolution
 
of
 
the
 
HGSC
 
Single-cell
 
Spatial
 
Atlas:
 
o
 
We
 
present
 
a
 
uniquely
 
large,
 
clinically
 
annotated,
 
multi-omics
 
dataset
 
of
 
280
 
HGSC
 
tumors,
 
integrating
 
genomics,
 
transcriptomics,
 
and
 
spatial
 
imaging
 
with
 
comprehensive
 
clinical
 
data.
 
o
 
Publicly
 
available,
 
clinically
 
and
 
molecularly
 
annotated
 
single-cell
 
spatial
 
dataset.
 
We
 
will
 
make
 
the
 
annotated
 
single-cell
 
spatial
 
data
 
with
 
molecular
 
profiles,
 
therapy
 
sequences,
 
and
 
clinical
 
survival
 
data
 
publicly
 
available.
 
This
 
will
 
be
 
an
 
extremely
 
valuable
 
resource
 
to
 
the
 
research
 
community
 
for
 
additional
 
hypothesis
 
generation
 
and
 
exploration.
 
o
 
Our
 
manuscript
 
provides
 
an
 
unparalleled
 
resource
 
and
 
conceptual
 
framework
 
for
 
understanding
 
tumor-immune
 
interactions
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
We
 
believe
 
that
 
our
 
findings
 
will
 
significantly
 
advance
 
efforts
 
in
 
biomarker
 
discovery,
 
therapeutic

standing
 
tumor-immune
 
interactions
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
We
 
believe
 
that
 
our
 
findings
 
will
 
significantly
 
advance
 
efforts
 
in
 
biomarker
 
discovery,
 
therapeutic
 
development,
 
and
 
patient
 
stratification,
 
with
 
broad
 
implications
 
for
 
cancer
 
research.
 
2.
 
Development
 
of
 
the
 
CEFIIRA
 
Machine-Learning
 
Pipeline:

o
 
CEFIIRA
 
is
 
a
 
robust
 
and
 
generalizable
 
machine-learning
 
tool
 
that
 
identifies
 
spatial
 
and
 
molecular
 
features
 
critical
 
for
 
tumor-immune
 
interactions.
 
It
 
incorporates
 
cross-validation,
 
feature
 
elimination,
 
and
 
feature
 
permutation
 
across
 
iterative
 
training
 
and
 
testing
 
cycles
 
to
 
minimize
 
bias
 
and
 
overfitting.
 
o
 
As
 
open-source
 
software,
 
CEFIIRA
 
enables
 
the
 
integration
 
of
 
multi-level
 
datasets,
 
creating
 
a
 
scalable
 
tool
 
for
 
cancer
 
research
 
and
 
other
 
diseases.
 
3.
 
Improved
 
Understanding
 
and
 
Clinical
 
Relevance
 
of
 
TME
 
spatial
 
structures
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
 
o
 
In
 
addition
 
to
 
tumor
 
compositions
 
and
 
immune
 
cell
 
infiltration,
 
we
 
present
 
a
 
systematic
 
analysis
 
of
 
the
 
tumor
 
spatial
 
domains
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
 
o
 
We
 
show
 
that,
 
particularly,
 
the
 
tumor-stroma
 
interface
 
composition
 
is
 
associated
 
with
 
distinct
 
clinical
 
outcomes.
 
 
o
 
Our
 
work
 
also
 
further
 
highlights
 
the
 
impact
 
of
 
the
 
tumor
 
molecular
 
profiles
 
and
 
chemotherapy
 
exposure
 
on
 
the
 
spatial
 
and
 
functional
 
TME.
 
Thus,
 
our
 
systematic
 
assessment
 
of
 
our
 
multiomics
 
dataset
 
brings
 
important
 
considerations
 
into
 
future
 
TME
 
analyses
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
4.
 
Discovery
 
of
 
Tumor-Intrinsic

ematic
 
assessment
 
of
 
our
 
multiomics
 
dataset
 
brings
 
important
 
considerations
 
into
 
future
 
TME
 
analyses
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
4.
 
Discovery
 
of
 
Tumor-Intrinsic
 
MHC-II
 
as
 
a
 
Critical
 
Biomarker:
 
o
 
We
 
demonstrate
 
that
 
spatially
 
heterogeneous,
 
tumor-intrinsic
 
MHC-II
 
expression
 
associates
 
with
 
clinical
 
outcomes
 
in
 
HGSC,
 
independently
 
of
 
the
 
clinicomolecular
 
subtypes.
 
o
 
Using
 
our
 
two
 
distinct
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
datasets,
 
we
 
show
 
that
 
tumor-intrinsic
 
MHCII
 
expression
 
is
 
associated
 
with
 
local
 
immune
 
infiltration
 
and
 
activation.
 
o
 
Using
 
mass
 
spectrometry
 
immunopeptidomics,
 
we
 
show
 
that
 
MHCII
 
binds
 
to
 
specific
 
immunopeptides
 
in
 
clinical
 
tumors.
 
This,
 
together
 
with
 
increased
 
TCR
 
clonality
 
associated
 
with
 
tumor-intrinsic
 
MHCII,
 
highlights
 
a
 
role
 
for
 
the
 
local
 
tumor
 
microenvironment
 
niches
 
in
 
the
 
overall
 
immunogenicity
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
o
 Using a functional patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor 
MHCII
 
expression
 
associates
 
with
 
enhanced
 
cytotoxicity
 
of
 
CD8+
 
T
 
cells
 
after
 
immune
 
checkpoint
 
inhibition.
 Altogether, our findings have significant clinical implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts 
overall
 
tumor
 
immunogenicity,
 
and

checkpoint
 
inhibition.
 Altogether, our findings have significant clinical implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts 
overall
 
tumor
 
immunogenicity,
 
and
 
guide
 
the
 
development
 
of
 
alternative
 
therapeutic
 
approaches
 
for
 
MHC-II–negative
 
HGSCs.
 Thank you for considering our manuscript for publication. We look forward to the opportunity to 
contribute
 
to
 
Cancer
 
Discovery
.
 Yours sincerely, 
 
 
Anniina
 
Färkkilä

MD
